title: Venous Blood pH Calculator
tagline: Analyze venous blood gas acid-base balance and classify acid-base disorders from VBG parameters.
intro: Enter pH, bicarbonate, PCO₂, base excess, temperature, and optionally hemoglobin to calculate the Henderson-Hasselbalch pH and classify the acid-base disorder.
formula: Formula: pH = 6.1 + log₁₀([HCO₃⁻] ÷ (0.0307 × PCO₂)) — Temperature correction: −0.015 per °C from 37°C

error.invalid: Please enter valid values for pH (6.5–8.0), bicarbonate, PCO₂, base excess, and temperature (30–45°C).

about.heading: About the venous blood pH calculator
about.body:
Blood gas analysis is one of the most important tools in clinical medicine for evaluating a patient's respiratory function and acid-base status. While arterial blood gas (ABG) measurements are considered the gold standard, venous blood gas (VBG) analysis provides a practical alternative when arterial sampling is difficult, painful, or contraindicated. Research has shown that venous pH correlates well with arterial pH (typically 0.03–0.05 pH units lower), making VBG a clinically useful substitute in many emergency and monitoring situations.

The pH of venous blood reflects the net balance between acid production and elimination throughout the body's peripheral tissues. Normal venous pH ranges from approximately 7.30 to 7.40, slightly lower than arterial blood because tissues release carbon dioxide and metabolic acids into the venous circulation. The key parameters measured in a VBG include: pH (hydrogen ion concentration expressed logarithmically), PCO₂ (partial pressure of carbon dioxide), HCO₃⁻ (bicarbonate, the main metabolic buffer), and base excess (the amount of acid or base that must be added to return the blood to normal pH at standard conditions).

The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation provides the theoretical relationship between these variables: pH = pKa + log([HCO₃⁻] / [H₂CO₃]), where pKa is the dissociation constant of carbonic acid (6.1) and [H₂CO₃] is approximated as 0.0307 × PCO₂. This calculator uses this equation to compute the expected pH from the entered HCO₃⁻ and PCO₂ values, then applies a Rosenthal temperature correction factor (−0.015 pH units per degree Celsius above 37°C) to account for samples analyzed at non-standard temperatures.

Classification of acid-base disorders follows four primary categories: metabolic acidosis (low pH, low HCO₃⁻, negative base excess), metabolic alkalosis (high pH, high HCO₃⁻, positive base excess), respiratory acidosis (low pH, elevated PCO₂), and respiratory alkalosis (high pH, reduced PCO₂). Mixed disorders occur when two primary processes coexist, such as combined metabolic and respiratory acidosis in septic shock. This calculator identifies the most likely primary disorder based on the entered values.

VBG analysis has been validated in emergency medicine for screening patients with diabetic ketoacidosis, sepsis, heart failure, and respiratory emergencies. A venous pH above 7.35 with a normal lactate effectively rules out severe acidosis in most clinical scenarios. Conversely, a markedly low venous pH (below 7.20) warrants urgent arterial blood gas confirmation and immediate clinical assessment.

This calculator is for educational and screening purposes. Interpretation of blood gas results requires integration with the clinical presentation, vital signs, oxygen saturation, and other laboratory data. Always involve a qualified clinician in any treatment decisions.

examples.heading: Venous blood gas examples
examples.intro: Click any example button to load preset VBG values into the calculator.

items.note: Normal venous blood gas in a healthy individual. All parameters within expected reference ranges.
Consistent with diabetic ketoacidosis. Low bicarbonate and very negative base excess drive the acidosis.
Hyperventilation pattern. Low PCO₂ with only mildly reduced bicarbonate suggests acute respiratory alkalosis.
Combined metabolic and respiratory acidosis, as may be seen in cardiorespiratory failure or severe septic shock.

howto.heading: How to use the venous blood pH calculator
steps: Obtain venous blood gas values from a peripheral venous sample or central venous catheter — record pH, HCO₃⁻, PCO₂, and base excess.
Enter the pH value (must be between 6.5 and 8.0 for a physiologically meaningful result).
Enter bicarbonate (mEq/L), PCO₂ (mmHg), and base excess (mEq/L) from the same VBG report.
Enter the sample temperature — defaults to 37°C if the analyzer used body temperature correction.
Optionally enter hemoglobin (g/dL) to estimate venous oxygen content, then click Calculate.

items.q: What is the normal pH of venous blood?
items.a: Normal venous blood pH ranges from approximately 7.30 to 7.40, which is slightly lower than arterial blood (7.35–7.45) due to carbon dioxide added by metabolizing tissues. A venous pH below 7.30 indicates significant acidosis; above 7.40 indicates alkalosis relative to venous norms.

items.q: How does venous pH compare to arterial pH?
items.a: Venous pH is typically 0.03–0.05 units lower than arterial pH, and venous PCO₂ is approximately 4–6 mmHg higher. Multiple validation studies have shown that a normal venous pH reliably excludes severe arterial acidosis in most patients, making VBG a useful screening tool before deciding whether an arterial blood gas is needed.

Base excess (BE) is the amount of acid or base, in mEq/L, that would need to be added to one liter of blood at standard conditions (37°C, PCO₂ 40 mmHg, 100% O₂ saturation) to return the pH to 7.40. A normal range is −2 to +2 mEq/L. Negative values indicate a metabolic acid load; positive values indicate a metabolic alkaline state.

items.q: When should a venous blood gas be used instead of arterial?
items.a: VBG is preferred when arterial access is difficult, painful, or risky — for example in patients with poor peripheral perfusion, coagulopathy, or when repeated sampling is needed. It is widely used in emergency departments for rapid triage of DKA, heart failure, and respiratory distress. ABG remains preferred when precise oxygenation assessment (PaO₂, SaO₂) is needed.

Common causes include diabetic ketoacidosis, lactic acidosis from sepsis or shock, renal tubular acidosis, poisoning with methanol or ethylene glycol, and severe diarrhea (bicarbonate loss). The anion gap helps distinguish high-anion-gap from normal-anion-gap metabolic acidosis and points toward the underlying cause.

items.q: Is this calculator a substitute for a clinical blood gas analyzer?
items.a: No. This tool uses the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation to estimate pH from entered values and classify the acid-base pattern for educational purposes. In clinical practice, blood gas values are measured directly by co-oximetry on a calibrated analyzer. All clinical decisions must be made by qualified healthcare professionals interpreting the full clinical picture.
